title: Examples
description: Markdown + Shiki examples (stored as JSON blocks)

blocks.text: Examples: **Markdown** formatting + `Shiki` code highlighting
This page demonstrates inline Markdown inside JSON fields: **bold**, *italic*, ~~strikethrough~~, `inline code`, and links like [Next.js](https://nextjs.org).
Tip: you can also paste a raw URL and GFM will autolink it: https://github.com/shikijs/shiki
Headings with formatting (TOC-safe)
A heading with **bold** and `code`
The right-side TOC strips common markdown markers so it stays readable.
Lists

items.text: Step 2: add a `code` block for examples

blocks.text: Tables (cells support inline Markdown)

rows: External links open in new tab

blocks.text: Code blocks (Shiki, light/dark themes)
Below are a few code blocks with different languages. Switch theme (top-right) to verify dual-theme highlighting.

blocks.code:
pnpm dev
pnpm build

# highlight should work in both light and dark themes

blocks.text: Quotes + callouts
You can format quotes too: **bold**, *italic*, `inline code`.
blocks.author: Wiki viewer

blocks.text: Markdown works in callouts: **success** + `inline` + [link](https://example.com).
Link block type
Link blocks are separate from markdown links; useful for call-to-action style links:
Go to **Getting Started** (label supports markdown, but no nested links)
Images (caption supports inline Markdown)
Cut blocks (collapsible sections)
Cut blocks create collapsible sections. They're perfect for hiding detailed content, FAQs, or optional information.
This content is hidden by default. Click the title to expand it. You can use **markdown** formatting inside cut blocks.
A cut can contain any type of content block:
You can even nest callouts inside cuts!

blocks.title: FAQ: How do I use cut blocks?

blocks.text: Cut blocks are defined in JSON with the following structure:

items.text: `blocks` - Array of content blocks to show when expanded

blocks.text: You can even nest cut blocks inside other cuts:
This is a nested cut block. It works just like the parent.
You can nest as deep as you need, though it's best to keep it reasonable for UX.
Steps (static mode with highlight)
Steps blocks help you create step-by-step guides. You can highlight specific steps to draw attention to them.

items.title: Install dependencies
items.description: Run `pnpm install` to install all required packages.

items.title: Configure environment
items.description: Create a `.env.local` file with your **API keys** and configuration.

items.title: Start development server
items.description: Run `pnpm dev` to start the development server on [localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000).

items.title: Build for production
items.description: When ready, run `pnpm build` to create an optimized production build.

blocks.text: Steps (dynamic mode)
Dynamic steps automatically highlight as you scroll through the page, creating an interactive reading experience.

items.title: Plan your architecture
items.description: Start by designing the overall structure of your application. Consider **scalability**, **maintainability**, and **performance**.

items.title: Set up your project
items.description: Initialize your project with the necessary tools and frameworks. Configure your build system and development environment.

items.title: Implement core features
items.description: Build the main functionality of your application. Focus on creating a solid foundation before adding advanced features.

items.title: Add styling and UI
items.description: Design and implement the user interface. Make sure it's **responsive** and provides a great user experience.

items.title: Test thoroughly
items.description: Write and run tests to ensure your code works correctly. Include unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests.

items.title: Deploy to production
items.description: Deploy your application to a production environment. Set up monitoring and logging to track performance and errors.
